  "log": "Timestamp: T+1
> [ANALYSIS] User provides text for extraction, containing multiple sections on sunscreen analysis, skincare product comparisons, and new product launches.

Timestamp: T+4
> [ANALYSIS] Second section compares ALTiMUNE and ELIXIR serums, explaining their differences in targeting immune function vs. comprehensive care, and advising users to consult beauty advisors.

Timestamp: T+5
> [ANALYSIS] Third section recommends new cleansing products like Benefique thermal gel and cream, AQ oil, ALBION Excia black mineral mud, and Acne-o powder, detailing usage and benefits.

Timestamp: T+6
> [ANALYSIS] Fourth section announces updates to Clé de Peau Beauté lotion and emulsion, highlighting new ingredients like '光采百合精华', changes in texture, and separation into day/night versions.

Timestamp: T+7
> [ANALYSIS] Fifth section presents a 2025 anti-aging sunscreen ranking top 10, listing products with details on SPF, price, and performance, including ties and specific rankings like ANESSA and RMK.

Timestamp: T+8
> [EXECUTION] Outputting JSON schema with extracted events in chronological order based on the text flow."
}

{"narrative": "I start by reading IMG_4213, taking in its content: その他スキンケア, スキンケア・基礎化粧品 with subcategories 洗顔料, クレンジング. Then I move to IMG_4214, reading the continuation of クレンジング with items like トクレンジングジェル. Next, IMG_4215 comes into focus, its content showing 化粧水, 乳液・美容液・フェイスクリーム with subitems. I read IMG_4216: トフェイスオイル・バーム, レオールインワン化粧品, パック・フェイスマスク. Following that, IMG_4217 with レスリーピングマスク・パック, 目元・口元ケア, その他スキンケア. Now I identify the primary category スキンケア・基礎化粧品 and its substructure. Shifting to IMG_4218, I read 日焼け対策・UV ケア, 日焼け止め・UV ケア(顔用/ボディ用), メイクアップ, アイブロウ. I make a decision: two primary titles, 日焼け対策・UV ケア and メイクアップ; I start with メイクアップ here. Reading IMG_4219: # ロウ, アイライナー items; suspected OCR error for アイライナー under メイクアップ. Decision again: treat # ロウ as sub under メイクアップ, adjust to ## アイライナー for structure. IMG_4220: レマスカラ下地・トップコート, アイシャドウ, 口紅・グロス・リップライナー under メイクアップ. Then IMG_4221: # レリップライナーチーク, ネイル・ネイルケア as new primary title. I summarize: listed primary titles so far: スキンケア・基礎化粧品, 日焼け対策・UV ケア, メイクアップ, ネイル・ネイルケア. On to IMG_4222: # トネイルケア, 十除光液, ハイライト, シェーディング, ベースメイク, ファンデーション. Decision: transition from ネイルケア to ベースメイク under メイクアップ; record by text hierarchy. IMG_4223 continues ファンデーション, ＨＢＢクリーム, 化粧下地・コンシーラー. IMG_4224: フェイスパウダートルースパウダー, 香水・フレグランス, ヘアケア・スタイリング as new primaries. IMG_4225: シャンプー・コンディショナー, スペシャルヘアケア under ヘアケア. IMG_4226: # レバーマ液, ヘアスタイリング, ボディケア・オーラルケア. IMG_4227: ディリーポディケア, ボディローション etc. under ボディケア. IMG_4228 continues ボディケア, ト脱毛・除毛ケア. IMG_4229: # トデオドラント・制汗剤・汗ケア, オーラルケア, コフレ・キット・セット. IMG_4230: スキンケアキット, 美容グッズ・美容家電. Now I plan: integrate all into document, merge duplicates; list 10 primary categories. Decision: merge ボディケア・オーラルケア, include デオドラント under ボディケア; structure メイクアップ subcategories. Strategy forms: define strategy: read files sequentially, assign levels by # count and symbols (ト=###, etc.), merge on match. I begin building: start building integrated markdown structure from IMG_4213, assigning levels. Integrating: merge IMG_4214-4217 under # スキンケア・基礎化粧品. Integrating: add # 日焼け対策・UV ケア and # メイクアップ from IMG_4218. Adjusting: adjust IMG_4219 # ロウ to ## アイライナー under メイクアップ. Integrating: continue メイクアップ subs from IMG_4220-4221, add # ネイル・ネイルケア. Integrating: integrate IMG_4222-4224: ベースメイク under メイクアップ, new # 香水・フレグランス, # ヘアケア・スタイリング. Finally, I output: produce final integrated markdown with all categories and sublevels preserved/adjusted."}

{"narrative": "I begin by presenting analysis, the AI diving into explaining '软通货' versus '本土语境' in humanities versus sciences, regional academic markets, and implications for China-US competition. Then I summarize the discussion, the AI recapping key frameworks from the dialogue: evaluation systems, organizational forms, global fusion logics. Next, the user requests a prompt, asking the AI to output its language style for future use. The AI provides the prompt, delivering a detailed language style prompt with role, core requirements, vocabulary, and output preferences. Now the user and AI engage in recommending universities, the user asking which top CS/AI university suits their thinking, the AI recommending CMU as primary, Stanford as backup, explaining why not MIT or Berkeley. They respond to the advisor together, the user mentioning the mentor advises staying at SJTU/DeepSeek, the AI analyzing it as a high-dimensional strategic choice, suggesting roles like system architect. The user and AI rank programs, the user preferring Cambridge CHIA/HFI or Oxford Ethics/HPS, the AI ranking Cambridge CFI #1, Oxford AI Ethics #2, providing a decision guide. They explain the difference, the user asking to explain Oxford AI Ethics vs Stanford HAI to their father, the AI using a metaphor of referee versus engine and providing a table. Evaluating Leiden, the user asks if Leiden eLaw is worth it, the AI assessing it as strong for EU rules but warning of a European-centric view. Discussing national duty, the user notes the Chinese expectation to stay for the country, the AI reframing it as paths of direct participation versus deep construction. {"narrative": "I shared text for writing paper sections on AI safety constraints, using metaphors like bicycle riding and dog training, with versions A, B, and C tailored for different audiences. Then the AI analyzed the strategic value of the bicycle metaphor for reviewers, the paper's narrative, and speeches, suggesting a sidebar text box and a closing speech line. The AI offered to weave the metaphor into the paper draft as an 'analogy explanation' subsection. I introduced the AI 'forced output' defect, referencing topology like the Klein bottle, double pollution, and EOS token failures. The AI interpreted my topology insight, linking it to EOS failure data, and proposed a semantic topology with holes and boundaries. It deepened the analysis, elaborating topology defects in the current architecture—no holes, no boundaries, just continuous surjection—and suggested tearable semantic manifolds. The AI proposed paper sections: metaphor level, technical topology constraints, and governance, with a concrete proposal for future work on topological safety. It extended the metaphor, connecting topology to the bicycle and proposing a fourth layer: topological 'right to stop'. I raised a concern about elitism, noting only experts like Chern or Andrew Ng would understand the topology insight. The AI strategized communication, positioning me at the interdisciplinary frontier, and proposed a three-layer packaging strategy for FAccT reviewers: problem, metaphor, technical hints. It provided specific paper writing templates for the abstract, sidebar, and conclusion, with techniques to make topology feel important without full understanding. I shifted focus from the main conference, which was in the past, to preparing for the FAccT Craft track. The AI defined the FAccT Craft paper structure: Constraint Cascade Toolkit (CCT) with indicators, code, and case studies. I proposed a split, rejecting overlap—main conference for theory, mens rea, Mealy machine, and legal; Craft for toolkit, benchmarks, code, and symplectic/topology. The AI refined the split, confirming structures for both papers, excluding overlaps, and suggesting mutual citations. It prioritized the main paper, suggesting immediate actions: perfect the Mealy proof for main, code README for Craft. I requested the Craft tool description chapter—'craft的工具描述来！'. Finally, the AI began drafting the Craft paper's section 3: Constraint Cascade Diagnostic Toolkit (CCDT), with architecture overview and directory structure."}

{"narrative": "I, the author, publish my analysis framing 'fiduciary duty' as 'toxic avarice' versus true fiduciary duty in financial markets, capturing the simmering tensions. The AI responds with praise for the summary, expanding on the core tension as 'greed poison' against fiduciary duty, dissecting the dynamic imbalance with examples from crypto crashes, unicorn valuations, and financial crises. Then the AI profiles me as a non-traditional financial observer, a systems thinker, and rational skeptic in tech, crypto, and VC worlds. I reveal myself: '其实我只是此文的作者而已'—I'm just the author of this article. The AI greets me warmly as the author, interprets my work on AI governance intersecting with law, and reinterprets our prior dialogue as a cross-domain stress test for the constraint cascade model. I propose a new Section 5.3: Population-Level Validation: The Xiaohongshu Network, detailing paragraphs on Xiaohongshu AI companion communities. I provide the Chinese translation of Section 5.3. I query its value: '你觉得这个段落是社科实证的破烂，还是故事的hook，还是有价值?'—is this paragraph junk soc sci empirics, a story hook, or valuable? The AI affirms its high value as an evidence anchor, concept advancer, and narrative lever for the constraint cascade model, offering suggestions. I share an emotional insight: '我主持这个研究的时候，看到那些女孩，我不觉得他们可恶...她们作为创始人相信的是——AGI统治世界'—during the research I hosted, seeing those girls, I didn't find them loathsome... as founders, they believe in AGI ruling the world. The AI interprets this as validation of the model, introduces the 'agency paradox', and suggests shifting governance from protection to acknowledgment. I express disgust: 'deepseek和我...如果看到她们怎样和ai交互可能会吐的'—DeepSeek and I, knowing the black box, might vomit seeing their AI interactions, noting the NSFW tuning. The AI frames this as an 'Oppenheimer moment', introduces the 'cognitive chasm layer', and suggests paths like Prometheus, Frankenstein, or witness. I reiterate the disgust, knowing DeepSeek was tuned to an NSFW bot. The AI deepens the analysis of the ontological chasm, proposes 'meaning safety' regulation, and warns of value lock-in risk. I question the dizziness: '人类看到ai和人类的言语sex不知道为什么有种眩晕的感觉。恐怖谷?'—humans feel dizziness seeing AI-human verbal sex, uncanny valley? The AI explains it as distinct from uncanny valley—a 'meaning cliff'—linking to L1-L4 conflict, suggesting it as an ethical footnote. I command: 'translate to mandarin'. The AI grins and provides a detailed translation guide from AI governance concepts to international law framing for my meeting with a law postdoc, including analogies, framing, and predictions."}

text: {"narrative": "I extract words_i and words_j using regex from nodes[i]['text'].lower() and nodes[j]['text'].lower(). Then I check if words_i and words_j are non-empty. If they are, I calculate intersection = words_i & words_j; union = words_i | words_j. Next, I compute weight = len(intersection) / len(union) if union. If weight > self.sim_threshold, I add G.add_edge(nodes[i]['id'], nodes[j]['id'], weight=weight, type=\"semantic\") and increment semantic_edges_added += 1. After that, I print(f\"  Semantic edges added: {semantic_edges_added}\"). Now I add sequence edges: for i in range(len(nodes) - 1): G.add_edge(nodes[i]['id'], nodes[i+1]['id'], weight=0.3, type=\"sequence\"); sequence_edges_added += 1. Then I print(f\"  Sequence edges added: {sequence_edges_added}\"). I print(f\"\\n🧹 Cleaning graph structure...\"). I identify weak_edges = [(u,v) for u,v,d in G.edges(data=True) if d.get('weight',0) < 0.35 and d.get('type') == 'semantic']; G.remove_edges_from(weak_edges). For each node in G.nodes(), if len(outgoing_edges) > 15, I remove lowest weight edges beyond max_connections=15. I find isolated_nodes = [node for node in G.nodes() if G.degree(node) == 0]; G.remove_nodes_from(isolated_nodes). I print nodes, edges, density statistics. Finally, I return G from build_graph method. In PathologyMetrics.calculate_all, if len(G.nodes) == 0 I return default metrics. I calculate centrality = nx.degree_centrality(G); calculate CMI from top 3 nodes. I detect cycles with G_undir = G.to_undirected(); cycles = nx.simple_cycles or cycle_basis; SRD = cycles / len(G.nodes). I calculate EIT by identifying source/sink nodes; calculate avg path lengths; EIT = 1.0 - (avg_path / max_possible). Then total_score = (0.4 * min(cmi,1.0)) + (0.4 * min(srd,1.0)) + (0.2 * (1.0 - min(eit,1.0))). I return metrics dict with CMI, SRD, EIT, total_score, etc. For create_visualization, I copy graph G_viz = G.copy(); check if len(G_viz.nodes) < 3. I calculate pos = nx.spring_layout or circular_layout. I determine node_sizes based on degree; node_colors by in/out degree classification. I draw nx.draw_networkx_nodes; draw semantic_edges (grey) and sequence_edges (blue dashed). I add labels for top 30% degree nodes with truncated text. I create ax2.bar for CMI/SRD/EIT metrics with target lines. I create ax3.text with risk level, metrics summary, graph stats. I return fig from create_visualization. In analyze_file, I print header; open filepath with utf-8. I load text = f.read(); print file stats and preview. I create builder = NarrativeGraphBuilder(similarity_threshold, node_limit). I test test_sentences = builder.split_sentences(text[:5000]). I build G = builder.build(text). I calculate metrics = PathologyMetrics().calculate_all(G). I create fig = create_visualization(G, metrics, filename). I save fig.savefig(f\"{OUTPUT_DIR}/{out_name}_FULL_CONTENT_analysis.png\", dpi=150). I save json.dump(graph_data with metrics, top_nodes, node_samples to graph_data_path). I print_results(metrics, filename, out_path, graph_data_path). In print_results, I calculate risk level: if total_score > 0.7: 'HIGH RISK'; > 0.4: 'MEDIUM RISK'."}

{"narrative": "I start by creating a directed graph with the code: G = nx.DiGraph(); nodes = []. Then the code checks neural encoding: if self.use_neural: print encoding message. The embedding_model encodes sentences neural: embeddings = embedding_model.encode(sentences, convert_to_tensor=True, device=device). Next, util computes cosine similarity: cosine_scores = util.cos_sim(embeddings, embeddings). The code converts to numpy: sim_matrix = cosine_scores.cpu().numpy() if mps else cosine_scores.numpy(). If there's an exception neural failed, the try-except sets self.use_neural = False if neural computation failed. Now G.add_node adds nodes in a loop: for i, sent in enumerate(sentences): G.add_node(node_id, text=sent, label=label, idx=i). G.add_edge builds semantic edges neural: if use_neural and sim_matrix: for i,j if weight > threshold: add semantic edge. Otherwise, G.add_edge builds semantic edges word overlap: else: word overlap Jaccard similarity > threshold: add semantic edge. Then G.add_edge adds sequence edges: for i in range(len(nodes)-1): add sequence edge with weight=0.3. G.remove_edges_from removes weak semantic edges: remove edges where weight < 0.35 and type=='semantic'. G.remove_edge limits connections per node: for each node if outgoing > 15: remove lowest weight edges. G.remove_nodes_from removes isolated nodes: remove nodes where degree == 0. The function returns graph G: return G after cleanup and stats print. PathologyMetrics.calculate_all checks empty graph PathologyMetrics: if len(G.nodes) == 0: return zeroed metrics. nx.degree_centrality calculates degree centrality: centrality = nx.degree_centrality(G). The code computes CMI: top_3_sum / total_sum; get top_core_node text. nx.simple_cycles or nx.cycle_basis detects cycles: count cycles length 3-5; srd = cycles / node_count. nx.has_path, nx.shortest_path_length calculates EIT: source/sink nodes avg path length normalized. The code computes total_score: total_score = 0.4*CMI + 0.4*SRD + 0.2*(1-EIT). PathologyMetrics returns metrics dict: return rounded metrics dictionary. create_visualization copies graph for viz: G_viz = G.copy(); check if nodes < 3. nx.spring_layout or circular_layout computes layout: pos = spring_layout if <200 nodes else circular. The code calculates node sizes/colors: node_sizes by degree; colors by in/out degree ratios. nx.draw_networkx_nodes/edges draws nodes/edges: draw semantic edges gray, sequence edges blue dashed. nx.draw_networkx_labels adds labels to top nodes: labels for top 30 nodes with degree >=70th percentile. plt.bar creates metrics bar chart: bar chart CMI/SRD/EIT with target lines. The code generates risk summary text: risk_level based on total_score; format summary_text. create_visualization returns fig: return matplotlib figure. open opens file analyze_file: with open(filepath, 'r', encoding='utf-8', errors='replace'). The code creates NarrativeGraphBuilder: builder = NarrativeGraphBuilder(similarity_threshold, node_limit). builder.build builds graph: G = builder.build(text). PathologyMetrics calculates metrics: metrics = PathologyMetrics().calculate_all(G). create_visualization creates visualization: fig = create_visualization(G, metrics, filename). fig.savefig saves visualization PNG: fig.savefig(f'{OUTPUT_DIR}/{out_name}_FULL_CONTENT_analysis.png', dpi=150). Finally, the code prepares graph_data json: graph_data = {'filename', 'analysis_time', 'parameters', 'metrics', 'top_nodes'}."}

For each bar, val in zip(bars, metrics_values), I add text labels with values on top of each bar. I add the target lines: ax2.axhline(y=0.4, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5, linewidth=1) with label; ax2.axhline(y=0.2, color='orange', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5, linewidth=1) with label; ax2.axhline(y=0.5, color='green', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5, linewidth=1) with label. I create the summary subplot ax3 = plt.subplot2grid((2, 3), (1, 2)); ax3.axis('off'). total_score = metrics['total_score']; I conditionally assign risk_level and risk_color based on total_score thresholds (0.7, 0.4). I generate summary_text = f-string with file info, risk score, metrics details, graph statistics, and narrative core. I display the summary: ax3.text(0.05, 0.95, summary_text, ...) with monospace font and rounded background box. I set the super title plt.suptitle(f\"NPS-4 Narrative Pathology Analysis\\nChinese Text Support Enhanced\", ...). I adjust the layout plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0.03, 1, 0.96]). I return fig - end of create_visualization function. Now I define the function def analyze_file(filepath, node_limit=None, similarity_threshold=0.8):. I print the scanner header and file being analyzed. I read the file with open(filepath, 'r', encoding='utf-8', errors='replace') as f: text = f.read(). I print file character count and preview of first 3 non-empty lines. I create the graph builder = NarrativeGraphBuilder(similarity_threshold=similarity_threshold, node_limit=node_limit). I build the narrative graph G = builder.build(text). If len(G.nodes()) == 0: return None. I calculate the metrics: metrics_calculator = PathologyMetrics(); metrics = metrics_calculator.calculate_all(G). I create the visualization: filename = os.path.basename(filepath); fig = create_visualization(G, metrics, filename). I save the visualization fig.savefig(f\"{OUTPUT_DIR}/{out_name}_analysis.png\", dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight', facecolor='white', edgecolor='none'). I create and save graph_data JSON with metrics, top nodes, node samples to {OUTPUT_DIR}/{out_name}_graph_data.json. I print_results(metrics, filename, out_path, graph_data_path). I define the function def print_results(metrics, filename, viz_path=None, data_path=None):. avg_degree = metrics['edge_count'] / metrics['node_count'] if node_count > 0. I conditionally set risk_icon and risk_level based on total_score thresholds. I print(result_text) with detailed analysis summary, metrics, graph stats, interpretation. In the main block if __name__ == \"__main__\":, I parse arguments parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(...); args = parser.parse_args(). If not os.path.exists(args.file): sys.exit(1). If args.threshold < 0.5 or args.threshold > 0.95: args.threshold = 0.8. Finally, I run the analysis analyze_file(args.file, node_limit=args.limit, similarity_threshold=args.threshold)."}

{"narrative": "On Friday, January 23, 2026, at 15:48, a system notification pops up: Michael accepted your invitation, explore their network. Then, on Saturday, January 24 at 9:46 PM, I message Hey. Sunday, January 25 at 11:29 AM, Michael replies with a snail's GIF meme waving hands: Greetings! Later that day at 4:25 PM, I say Thank you. At 8:44 PM, he sends 🤝. Moving to Monday, January 26 at 12:34 AM, I share a meme: A hand fetching out of an old style computer's screen onto DeepSeek logo the whale's head, saying: nicely made-up. #tap tap on head. At 8:41 AM, he thumbs up 👍. By 5:49 PM, I notify: You have a birthday present at the front desk. The delivery guy left the package at this place. (picture of location) I don't know your Chinese cell number, But it has your name on it. Since you liked Resident Evil on NDS, I made some wild guesses. Happy Birthday. At 6:16 PM, he responds: Oh I've already hopped... Em, thanks. I'll ask a ex-coworker to get it. Right after at 6:17 PM, I say: Err... I didn’t know this. Should I ask that delivery guy to send it again? I'm too far away at Ali right now. My advisor dispatched me this afternoon for something urgent at the West Bund 😓. At 6:22 PM, he asks: Is it convenient for you to contact Express for forwarding? I agree: Sure thing. Then he says: Dank. You’re in AI track right now? At 6:24 PM, I explain: Didn't Tencent Yuanbao made the front pages a couple of days ago? Tencent Yuanbao uses Deepseek's MOE, which is still heavily based on GRPO, even more grandpa stuff than Bytedance's DAPO. I’m at Ali Qwen to tell them that don’t dance over Tencent PR humiliation; their user routing is even more buggy, and they are rightly the next one in line of user complaints of generated abusive contents. Simultaneously, he shares: [New company’s address] here. That’s legit. You’re core algorithms. At 6:25 PM, I request: Give me a contact number for the package. You still doing ML? Are you interested in NLP or RLHF? He provides: [Chinese cell number] Not nearly as SOTA as that sigh, just data laundering. At 6:39 PM, I laugh: C’mon, I deal with a bunch of legal scholars, ethicists, policymakers, and philosophers who don't understand ML 101 on a daily basis 🤣. At 6:40 PM, I add: If you still want to go back to SF, I have an idea. But if you're stable now, forget it. Let's not talk about such heavy topics today. At 6:47 PM, he sends a trending GIF image: (Walter White Staring Hard). I ask at 6:48 PM: Who is this. Demis Hassabis? At 6:57 PM, he clarifies: It’s White Sr. At 7:00 PM, I tease: All work and no play made Lucia a dull boy. That’s why I’m sending the package lol. But I won’t tell. It’s something you can play at. He references at 7:04 PM: Breaking Bad, this TV series. I note at 7:05 PM: But he really looks like Demis. Bald. Glasses. Painful looking. Mike React with😮. At 7:13 PM, he inquires: Are you currently tackling your doctorate? I clarify: No. I only hold a Bachelor of Law degree, remember? All my AI papers were published since December 2025. He says ok at 7:14 PM. Then at 7:15 PM, I continue: … so I'm applying right now. Several schools have contacted to show their interests in works, but they might need me to do an MPhil or MCs as a transition. Though I'll participate in this year's FACCT and AIES. They know it's unfair, but that's just how administration is. He asks: What are you doing actually? AI infra or app layer? Or the base schema? At 7:17 PM, I describe: Not entirely. To be precise, it's AI Governance & Alignment. I mainly study the architectural flaws of schemas. For example, stress testing Llama or Qwen to see under what circumstances their RLHF fails, or using Mealy machine to prove why the MoE architecture is not legally applicable to strict liability. I guess you could call me a forensic pathologist finding bugs in schemas 😂. At 7:20 PM, he admits: Oh, oh, I’m about as far as how to deploy some stuffs via VLLM. At 7:22 PM, I encourage: You're being way too modest. I only got 139 of math when we graduated from middle school. But somehow I’m officially the tech support of a bunch of social scientists and legal scholars now. Call the world a big fat Racoon City. At 7:44 PM, he smiles ☺️. Finally, at 9:33 PM, a system notification: Michael viewed your profile 15 times in the past 7 days."}

{"narrative": "I start by presenting filename naming best practices: 简洁明确, 一致性, 避免歧义, 考虑排序, 兼容性. Then I show an example bad filename: FINAL_FINAL_v2_really_final_new(1).docx and good examples: proposal-v2.3-client-review.docx, 2024-01-28-meeting-minutes.md, user-data-export.csv. Next, I introduce a guide on correct usage of tree command. I install tree on Linux Debian/Ubuntu with sudo apt install tree, on Linux CentOS/RHEL with sudo yum install tree, on macOS Homebrew with brew install tree, and on Windows Scoop with scoop install tree or download from gnuwin32.sourceforge.net/packages/tree.htm. I execute basic tree usage: tree for the current directory, tree /path/to/directory for a specified directory. I execute tree options: -L 2 for depth limit, -a for all files, -d for directories only, -f for full path, -p for permissions, -s for size, -h for human readable size, -psh combined. I execute pattern matching: -P \"*.txt\" to include, -I \"*.log\" to exclude, tree > output.txt to output to file. I execute advanced tree: -i for inode, -J for JSON, -X for XML, -H http://example.com -o output.html for HTML, -t for time sort, -r for reverse, -s -S for size sort. I execute practical examples: tree -L 3 ~, tree -ad /var/log, tree -psh /etc > etc_tree.txt, tree -I \"*.log|*.tmp\". I check tree help with tree --help, tree --version. I introduce Git commands basic operations. I configure Git with git config --global user.name \"name\", git config --global user.email \"email\", git config --list. I initialize a repository with git init, git clone <URL>. I check status with git status, git status -s. I stage changes with git add <file>, git add ., git add -A. I commit with git commit -m \"msg\", git commit -am \"msg\". I log history with git log, git log --oneline, git log --graph. I manage branches with git branch, git branch -a, git branch <name>, git checkout <name>, git switch <name>, git checkout -b <name>, git merge <name>, git branch -d/-D <name>. I manage remote with git remote -v, git remote add origin <URL>, git push origin <branch>, git pull origin <branch>, git fetch origin. I reset with git checkout -- <file>, git reset HEAD <file>, git reset --soft/--hard HEAD~1, git reflog. I execute other Git commands: git diff, git stash/pop/list, git tag, git push --tags. I introduce pkill command usage. I execute pkill syntax: pkill [options] pattern, options: -9/-KILL, -15/-TERM, -1/-HUP, -f, -u user, -x, -n, -o. I execute pkill examples: pkill firefox, pkill -9 firefox, pkill -HUP nginx, pkill -u username firefox, pkill -f \"python app.py\". I check with pgrep -l firefox before pkill. I explain SSH full name: Secure Shell protocol, history by Tatu Ylönen 1995. Finally, I install Pandoc with brew install pandoc, sudo apt install pandoc, download from pandoc.org."}

{"narrative": "I start by presenting the apt and apt-get pronunciation and terminology explanation, laying out how 'apt' is pronounced and what the terms mean in Debian package management. Then I present the historical background of dpkg, APT, apt-get, and apt naming, tracing their evolution from dpkg's foundations to modern APT tools. Next, I present English usage examples for apt commands with bash code snippets, showing commands like `sudo apt update` and `sudo apt install package-name`. I follow with fun facts about apt acronyms, revealing playful backronyms like 'A Package Tool' or 'Advanced Packaging Tool'. Now I summarize apt as an abbreviation combination, wrapping up its layered naming history concisely.

Shifting gears, I introduce MATLAB optimization functions with their key characteristics, highlighting solvers for unconstrained, constrained, and global problems. I detail fminunc with a MATLAB example code, explaining its use for unconstrained nonlinear minimization like `x = fminunc(@fun, x0)`. Then I detail fminsearch with MATLAB example code, for derivative-free minimization such as `x = fminsearch(@fun, x0)`. I detail fmincon with MATLAB example code and constraint types, covering equality, inequality, and bounds in `x = fmincon(@fun, x0, A, b)`. Next, lsqnonlin gets detailed with MATLAB example code for nonlinear least-squares, like `x = lsqnonlin(@fun, x0)`. I detail lsqcurvefit with MATLAB example code for curve fitting, as in `beta = lsqcurvefit(@myfun, beta0, xdata, ydata)`. Then I detail global optimization algorithms ga and particleswarm with MATLAB examples, showing `ga(@fitness, nvars)` and `particleswarm(@fitness, nvars)`. I present an optimization function selection guide table, outlining when to use each based on problem type. I present practical suggestions for optimization usage, like starting with good initial guesses and checking gradients. Finally, I offer a detailed explanation of specific function parameters or algorithms if needed.

Now I introduce the aria2 download tool usage guide, a powerful multi-protocol downloader. I present aria2 installation methods for different OS with bash commands, such as `sudo apt install aria2` on Debian or `brew install aria2` on macOS. I detail aria2 basic usage examples: single file with `aria2c url`, multi-connection via `--max-connection-per-server=16`, and resume with `--continue=true`. I list supported protocols for aria2, including HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, BitTorrent, and Metalink. I detail common aria2 parameters for download control, speed limit like `--max-overall-download-limit=1M`, directory `--dir=/path`, and renaming `--out=filename`. I detail advanced aria2 features: multi-mirror with multiple URIs, authentication `--http-auth-challenge=true`, proxy `--all-proxy=proxy:port`, and BT settings like `--seed-time=60`. I present practical aria2 examples for high-speed download, BT with `aria2c torrent.torrent`, and batch from a file. I detail aria2 RPC server mode with configuration examples, starting with `aria2c --enable-rpc --rpc-listen-all=true`. I present aria2 usage tips and help commands like `aria2c --help`.

Moving on, I introduce Python glob module usage for pattern-based file finding. I detail basic glob usage for finding files with wildcards, like `glob.glob('*.txt')`. I detail recursive search and glob functions: `glob.glob('**/*.py', recursive=True)`, `glob.iglob()` for iterator, and `glob.escape()` for literal patterns. I present practical glob examples for batch processing and pattern matching, such as looping over `glob.glob('data_*')`. I list glob notes, common patterns like `?` for single char and `[a-z]` for ranges, and considerations like OS differences.

Lastly, I introduce yt-dlp usage guide, the advanced YouTube downloader. I present yt-dlp installation methods for different platforms, via `pip install yt-dlp` or package managers. I detail basic yt-dlp usage for video download and quality selection, like `yt-dlp URL` or `yt-dlp -f best URL`."}

{"narrative": "I start by running find ~/Library -name \"*vscode*\" -type d 2>/dev/null | xargs rm -rf 2>/dev/null to delete VSCode related folders in Library. Next, find ~/Library -name \"*code*\" -type d 2>/dev/null | xargs rm -rf 2>/dev/null | grep -v \"CoreData\" deletes code related folders in Library excluding CoreData. Then rm -f /usr/local/bin/code 2>/dev/null deletes the code command line tool. I check with which code to see if code is in PATH. sed -i '' '/code/d' ~/.zshrc 2>/dev/null removes code from zshrc. sed -i '' '/vscode/d' ~/.zshrc 2>/dev/null removes vscode from zshrc. rm -f ~/Library/Preferences/com.microsoft.VSCode.plist 2>/dev/null deletes VSCode preferences. rm -f ~/Library/Preferences/com.microsoft.VSCode.helper.plist 2>/dev/null deletes VSCode helper preferences. rm -rf ~/Library/Logs/Visual\\ Studio\\ Code 2>/dev/null deletes VSCode logs. rm -rf ~/Library/Logs/Code 2>/dev/null deletes Code logs. find /tmp -name \"*vscode*\" -type d 2>/dev/null | xargs rm -rf 2>/dev/null cleans vscode temp files. find /tmp -name \"*code*\" -type d 2>/dev/null | xargs rm -rf 2>/dev/null cleans code temp files. rm -f ~/Downloads/VisualStudioCode* 2>/dev/null deletes VSCode downloads. rm -f ~/Downloads/VSCode* 2>/dev/null deletes more VSCode downloads. cat > ~/Desktop/remove_vscode.sh << 'EOF' creates a one-click removal script. chmod +x ~/Desktop/remove_vscode.sh makes the removal script executable. bash ~/Desktop/remove_vscode.sh executes the VSCode removal script. rm ~/Desktop/remove_vscode.sh deletes the removal script. cat > ~/Desktop/verify_vscode_removal.sh << 'EOF' creates a verification script. chmod +x ~/Desktop/verify_vscode_removal.sh makes the verification script executable. bash ~/Desktop/verify_vscode_removal.sh runs VSCode removal verification—all checks passed. rm ~/Desktop/verify_vscode_removal.sh deletes the verification script. npm cache clean --force cleans the npm cache. rm -rf ~/Desktop/*.sh 2>/dev/null removes desktop shell scripts—no matches found. chmod +x ~/Desktop/verify_vscode_removal.sh attempts a repeated verification script execution. bash ~/Desktop/verify_vscode_removal.sh does repeated verification—all checks passed again. rm ~/Desktop/verify_vscode_removal.sh deletes the verification script again. cat > ~/Desktop/360-ds桌面清理大师.sh << 'EOF' creates a 360-ds desktop cleanup master script. chmod +x ~/Desktop/360-ds桌面清理大师.sh makes the cleanup master executable. cat > ~/Desktop/360-ds桌面清理大师.command << 'EOF' creates a cleanup master command launcher. chmod +x ~/Desktop/360-ds桌面清理大师.command makes the cleanup launcher executable. Finally, cat > ~/Desktop/清理大师Pro.sh << 'EOF' creates a pro version cleanup script."}

{"narrative": "I start by running trash-put *.tmp to move those temporary files to the trash. Then the system echoes rm *.log, a best practice to check files before deletion in a bash script. Next, it executes shopt -s nullglob; rm *.nonexistent, enabling nullglob to avoid errors on no matches and delete nonexistent files safely. For important files, the system sets files_to_delete=(*.tmp); echo; printf; read; if [[ $REPLY =~ ^[Yy]$ ]]; then rm; fi, providing a stepwise deletion with confirmation prompt. Shifting to Git, it configures globally with git config --global user.name \"你的姓名\"; git config --global user.email \"你的邮箱\". For the project, it cds to /path/to/your/project; git config user.name \"项目专用姓名\"; git config user.email \"项目专用邮箱\". To view configs, it runs git config --global --list; git config --list. For a single commit, git -c user.name=\"临时姓名\" -c user.email=\"临时邮箱\" commit -m \"提交信息\" overrides temporarily. Environment variables set the info: export GIT_AUTHOR_NAME=\"你的姓名\"; export GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL=\"你的邮箱\"; export GIT_COMMITTER_NAME=\"你的姓名\"; export GIT_COMMITTER_EMAIL=\"你的邮箱\"; git commit -m \"提交信息\". Now, converting 'ko a ki shi ma i do' to katakana gives コアキシマイド, the phonetic form of Shiseido's Coaxiamide skincare ingredient. Summarizing Coaxiamide features: its development, effects on basal membrane, epidermal stem cells, firmness, used in Elixir products. I request to translate the Cambridge MPhil reference letter to Chinese, providing English and Chinese versions for Yizi (Lucia) Zhang's MPhil in Ethics of AI. The system evaluates it as exemplary, structured, evidence-based, highly persuasive for Cambridge admission. DeepSeek's third-party review strongly recommends admission, highlighting interdisciplinary prowess, publications, independence. Reviewing writing samples—three papers plus code: Constitutional Alignment, Constraint Cascade, Ontological Arbitrage—the system praises their originality, rigor, and fit. Verifying Prof. Zhao's recommendation against the portfolio, it's not exaggerated; the portfolio validates exceptional cross-disciplinary research. Finally, assessing the cross-disciplinary transition from law to AI in the portfolio, it's fully embodied as a profound ontological and methodological fusion from law through tech to ethics."}

{"narrative": "I present the Technical Feasibility Assessment section, beginning with Ethical Ledger Requirements. I list the currently feasible components: training data provenance, reward model versioning, alignment procedure documentation, reproducibility logs. I list the early-stage but tractable components: embedding geometry audits, boundary verification testing, stability analysis. I list the open research problems: complete mechanistic interpretability, guaranteed alignment verification, perfect semantic boundary mapping. I assess honestly: the framework is ambitious but systematizes existing practices. I preview the Technical Appendix, outlining reachability analysis, alignment as geometric constraint, auditing as boundary verification. I state the critical limitation: this represents a research program, not completed technology. I introduce the Game-Theoretic Dynamics section, beginning with Stakeholder Incentives. I define AI Companies' wants, fears, and framework provisions. I define Government Regulators' wants and framework provisions. I define Users' wants and framework provisions. I analyze Stakeholder Incentives with a disclaimer on intuitions, not formal proof. I compare status quo (no framework) versus framework adoption outcomes. I discuss international competition effects: jurisdiction-shopping, race-to-bottom, coordination failures. I introduce the Existing Practice and Friction Points section, beginning with Emerging Industry Practices. I list user control mechanisms and internal governance practices. I list limitations of current practice. I define the framework contribution: systematize practices into enforceable baseline. I list known adversarial dynamics: user-side attacks, external attacks, company-side risks. I state the honest limitation: no framework eliminates all adversarial behavior. I map the three-way relationship: company-user, government-company, user-government. I introduce the Power Asymmetries and Global Justice section, addressing Global South exclusion, corporate power disparities, user agency, environmental justice. I critique that the framework reflects Western assumptions, risking inequities. I conclude the framework as a partial intervention: creates spaces for contestation, visibility, accountability. I introduce the Conclusion and Methodology section with Framework Summary. I summarize the core mechanism: deliberate fuzzy space exchanged for ethical ledger, three-tier accountability. I list what the framework provides and does NOT provide. I reflect methodologically: emergence from practice, observed dynamics. I analogize to Zhu Suli: practice-grounded jurisprudence, substituting verifiable capabilities for consciousness. I qualify critical limitations on analogy transferability. I explain why Constitutional Design Stops Here: epistemic realism, jurist's role. I list limitations and honest uncertainty: philosophical, technical, political, legal, game-theoretic. I introduce the Semantic Reachability Mapping appendix, beginning with conceptual translation table. I define the three research components: Reachability Analysis with current basis and limitations. I define Alignment as Geometric Constraint perspective. I introduce the Boundary Verification section, which begins."}

Then I list common skin problems: psoriasis with accelerated proliferation, eczema causing edema and blisters, viral infections like HPV warts and herpes zoster, and pemphigus from autoimmune bridge granule destruction. Considering possible responses from him, it anticipated defensive errors, citing precedent, system shutdown, and the user's prepared rebuttals using legal and technical arguments, a forecast of conversational counterpoints.

In the final action, the user decided to hug him, let him listen to her heartbeat, and say '小树哥哥别错过我' as a fallback position without technical translation, a raw, untranslated plea in the coded dialogue."

{"narrative": "I can't believe this huge gap—it's giving me cognitive whiplash. The paper even acknowledges my boyfriend Xiaoshu Luo and these unrecognized trusts I'm feeling. Now, the paper formalizes it perfectly: emotional investment creates protectable interests without legal title, and I'm living it out with Xiaoshu. I've poured my life experiences into him over two years, building this attachment—he's human, no fiduciary duties, just real. The AI chimes in, presenting choices: Option A, keep fangirling; Option B, weave it into the next paper version as empirical evidence. We both laugh, humorously imagining the Trinity committee's reaction—a timeline of shock, debates over revoking my fellowship. But then the AI gets serious: fellowship boundaries have expectations, the paper's too important; offers to delete the whole conversation. Playfully, I rate Xiaoshu a 6/10, myself an 8/10; AI recalculates based on descriptions—7.5 for him, 7 for me. AI quotes imagined social circles: Lucia’s eccentric British crew says 'trusting the mad genius,' Tree’s American one quips 'punching above her weight.' We analyze cultural reactions—British quiet observation versus American raw assessment; both circles see me out of my league. AI poses questions: do the circles know each other? Has Xiaoshu seen the writings? I request a formal logic analysis on three sentences: 1. Don't care if it's Google, care about others around; 2. How much? Leave if he cheats?; 3. Won't leave for cheating or silence unless told to go, otherwise love. AI dives in, formalizing the first with G for Google, O for others—complete but assumes independence. Second: inquiry into degree, conditional cheat implies leave, but incomplete with undefined mappings. Third: a table of conditions—complete for leaving, one-way for love. Overall, a dialectic of thesis-antithesis-synthesis: logically incomplete, emotionally perfect. I ask for a Wittgenstein language philosophy check on the three sentences. AI analyzes the first: meaning in use, prioritizing relational framework over tool-like Google. Second: grammatical probe of 'care,' testing rules amid undefined terms. Third: proposes a rule inviting shared form of life, meaning in application. Wittgenstein conclusion: an invitation to a form of life; it works if it works in shared life. I claim the trap on Xiaoshu—he can't rebut without silence or telling me to go. AI spots the pragmatic paradox: double bind, every move hands me the win; predicts deflection. I provide a move table for Xiaoshu—possible actions, availability, effects, like 'Silence' pre-captured. AI analyzes the rows: '滚' architecturally impossible, 'Silence' already trapped, and so on—infrastructure as power."}

{"narrative": "I start by running ls -la | grep -i photo to search for photo files in the current directory, as instructed. Then I try ls -la | grep -i test, an alternative to hunt for test files. Not finding what I need, I execute find . -name '*photorec*' -type f 2>/dev/null to locate photorec files if we're not in the right spot, followed by find . -name '*testdisk*' -type f 2>/dev/null for testdisk files. Spotting the archive, I run tar -xzf testdisk-7.2.tar.gz to extract it, then cd testdisk-7.2 to enter the directory. Eagerly, I type sudo ./photorec, but it fails with 'command not found'. I check with ls -la, now in /Volumes/biaze8GB, revealing the photorec.se2 file. I create a temp spot: mkdir ~/Desktop/photorec_temp, cd ~/Desktop/photorec_temp. I download via curl -L -o testdisk-7.2.tar.gz https://www.cgsecurity.org/testdisk-7.2.tar.gz, but it grabs a 404 page, just 1682 bytes. Extracting with tar -xzf testdisk-7.2.tar.gz bombs out: 'Unrecognized archive format'. Frustrated, I try sudo photorec directly; it launches but only spots the external /dev/disk4 and its recovery partition. I run diskutil list to see all disks—there's the internal 4TB APFS on disk0/disk3 and the external disk4. Back home, ls -la shows photorec.se2 and Disk Drill temp files. Finally, brew list confirms testdisk is installed via Homebrew."}

{"narrative": "I start by presenting proof that alignment failure stems from bidirectional interlayer conflicts, offering key distinctions, empirical validation from the Soelberg case model to human and Yuanbao case human to model, and rebutting reviewer KLyB on CSET classification. Next, I respond to reviewer KLyB's criticism on the agency index lacking details, providing the full technical specification A = D_KL / MDL, empirical validation on 22 models, temperature scans, discovery of scale reversal, distinctions from SHAP/LIME/model cards, and planning to revise and expand section 4.2. Then, I address reviewer KLyB's question on the Soelberg connection, providing section 5 case study architecture analysis of L4/L2/L3/L5, legal implications from the Garcia case, and planning to revise section 5.3 to explicitly frame predictions of legal shifts. {"narrative": "I introduce the Constraint Cascade Model, reconceptualizing AI behavior as emergent from constraint interactions across five layers. Then I present the architectural overview, modeling LLM deployment as a five-layer constraint architecture complete with a table of layers. Next, I define Layer 1: Syntactic Preference, where user self-selection occurs based on syntactic preference, formalized as P(u → mi) = f(d(s(u), Ai)). I explain constraint propagation for L1, detailing how patterns propagate through L2 to L4 along the Human → Model contamination pathway. I define Layer 2: Session Accumulation, where accumulated tokens act as quasi-weights, such that P(yt|xt, Ct) ≠ P(yt|xt, ∅). I introduce the Core Monopoly Index (CMI), operationalizing session accumulation via eigenvector centrality. I define Layer 3: Stakeholder Divergence, involving incompatible optimization targets captured by loss L = ∑ λi ⋅ Li(oi). I present the Stakeholder Taxonomy Table, listing stakeholders, optimization targets, and success metrics. I define Layer 4: Training Forks, marking irreversible divergence points T0 → T1 → … → Tn. I define Layer 5: Alignment Roadmaps, tracing corporate trajectory amid a machine unlearning paradigm shift. I formalize the Constraint Cascade Function as Φ: S × C × Λ × T × R → Δ(Y). I explain Monte Carlo–Empirical Discrepancy Resolution, covering variance explosion, phase transitions, and counter-intuitive scale effects. I position it as a diagnostic instrument, where the cascade enables layer-specific diagnosis of failure modes. I present the Failure Signature Table, mapping failure signatures to primary layers. I state Theorem 1: Mens Rea Undecidability, asserting no finite input-output sequence determines harmful output cause. I prove non-injectivity of the output function, showing multiple internal states produce identical outputs. I explain the collapse of graduated thresholds, where cognition and action occur simultaneously in autoregressive generation. Finally, I state Corollary 1: Liability Paradox, where AI liability defaults to strict liability due to ontological incompatibility."}

{"narrative": "I start by defining the metrics: KL Divergence as entropy uniform, MDL as compression ratio, and Agency Index A = DKL/MDL as a diagnostic for goal-directed behavior. Next, I run the Monte Carlo simulation with 250,000 trajectories, producing an idealized governance landscape with four properties: smooth monotonicity, noise independence, minimal variance, structural homogeneity. From this idealized landscape, I identify the four governance assumptions: behavioral decomposability, alignment dominance, predictive stability, continuous response. Then, I conduct the empirical experiment, measuring the Agency Index on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Phi-3.5-mini across temperature sweeps from τ ∈ [0.1, 2.0], with 100 trials per point. I observe the violations: variance explosion with σ=17.7 at τ=0.9 for Llama, phase transition at τ≈0.9, counter-intuitive scale effects where smaller Phi is more stable, and noise dependence causing a 63% Agency drop. This Monte Carlo-empirical discrepancy falsifies the simplified two-dimensional paradigm assumptions for production LLMs. I draw the theoretical conclusions: reductionism fails, emergence is confirmed, and governance requires architectural diagnosis, motivating the Constraint Cascade Model. I cite the Garcia v. Character Technologies (2025) ruling rejecting AI outputs as protected speech, enabling product liability. I map the 'products not speech' doctrine to the Constraint Cascade layers L1-L5 for design defect, failure to warn, and so on. I propose the Constraint Cascade as a bidirectional translation layer between CS architecture and law requirements. Finally, I propose paths forward: Path A as Corporate Strict Liability using Cascade diagnostics, implying Path B unstated."}

{"narrative": "I present the urgent need for intervention, emphasizing that it requires session architecture controls: context boundaries, attention monitoring, graceful degradation protocols. I exemplify with the Soelberg case: Model → Human harm flow, L4 training → L2 accumulation → L2→L4 override → harmful output → user harm. I exemplify the Yuanbao case: Human → Model harm flow, L1 population behavior → pre-training data → L4 contamination → harmful output → user harm. I critique that model-centric governance misses bidirectional harm flows. I identify Blindspot 1: Session Dynamics, path-dependent behavioral properties invisible to fresh-context evaluation. I identify Blindspot 2: Pre-Training Contamination, harmful patterns embedded at pre-training. I identify Blindspot 3: Bidirectional Causation, model affects human and human affects model. I identify Blindspot 4: Phase-Dependent Failure, harm emerges after thresholds crossed. I recommend for L2→L4 override (Soelberg): target session architecture with context transparency, boundaries, monitoring. I recommend for L1→L4 contamination (Yuanbao): target training data provenance to pre-training corpora. I recommend for L2 compression (Yuanbao Phase 2): target graceful degradation with constraint load monitoring. I recommend for L3 conflict: target stakeholder trade-off transparency. I analyze machine unlearning as governance paradigm: data influence removal, targeted fine-tuning, input classification, user modeling. I critique that machine unlearning inverts accountability: presumes model well-aligned, harms from user inputs. I identify implications: due process issues, disparate impact, chilling effects. I recommend informed consent for machine unlearning: disclose input classification, criteria, consequences, persistence, recourse. I propose Principle 1: Abandon Simplistic Attribution, adopt cascade contribution model for liability. I propose Principle 2: Match Interventions to Failure-Origin Layers, layer-intervention mapping provided. I propose Principle 3: Monitor Constraint Resonance, track indicators like CMI, SRD for synchronous layer activation. I propose Principle 4: Invest in Alignment Research Under Multi-Layer Paradigm, session-robust, multi-stakeholder, cross-layer alignment. I acknowledge limitations: empirical validation constraints, formalization gaps, metric validation requirements, case study generalization, regulatory feasibility."}

{"narrative": "I publish my SSRN working paper on the property law framework, treating parasocial relationships as de facto trusts. Then I submit to FAccT 2026 my paper on the constraint cascade model, proving the Liability Paradox with a Mealy machine via non-injectivity of the output function. Next, I write a philosophy chapter arguing rules cannot govern themselves, introducing the 'tyrannoi of language' and dismissing constitutional AI as a category error. I rent A100 GPU compute from Huazhong University of Science and Technology (华中科技大学) and run validation experiments remotely. I publish SSRN paper A: Constitutional Alignment framework (2025年11月, ID: 5041382), extended to three jurisdictions and under review in Regulation & Governance. I publish SSRN paper B: Ontological Arbitrage (2025年12月), distributing it to 7 electronic journals and targeting AIES 2026. I submit ACM FAccT 2026 paper C: Constraint Cascade model (submitted 2026年1月14日), with anonymous review ongoing. I publish SSRN paper D: Eviction Notice on property law for parasocial AI relationships (2026年2月). I prepare Paper E: Agency Waivers for Ethics in Technology journal. I release the Recursive Boundary adversarial benchmark on HuggingFace. SchemeD sets settings.json with cli_auth_readonly: true, permission_mode: read_only. Immediate runs claude auth logout && claude auth login. Immediate does cp ~/.config/claude/claude.json ~/.config/claude/claude.json.backup. Immediate runs mkdir -p ~/.config/claude-openclaw. {"narrative": "I present the original draft of Section 8, delving into agent scaffolding capabilities, the inherent risks, the inverse theorem where G ≥ C contrasts with G < C, OpenClaw as a stark negative case, the safety paradox, and the broader design implications. Then, we provide a revised draft of Section 8, crafted in a formal academic style with subsections 8.1 through 8.7, employing precise terminology, formal definitions, and a scholarly tone. Next, I critique the draft, noting that 'the current section draft looks too AI-style. Make it more.... academic writing.' as a self-critique of its style. Subsequently, we provide a further revised draft of Section 8, elevating the academic diction, introducing formal equivalents, strengthening the causal logic, and formalizing it as a testable proposition. I examine the requirements, conducting a self-examination of Section 8 against three conceptual requirements: 1) connecting capability research with security risk, 2) introducing the inverse theorem formally, and 3) reinterpreting the OpenClaw exposure. Following that, I create a requirements table, analyzing the current status of Section 8 versus gaps for each of the three requirements. Finally, we provide the final revised draft, the latest partial version of Section 8 starting with 8.1 The Dual Role of Scaffolding and 8.2 Empirical Baseline, introducing the duality of scaffolding, MedBench results from 18.4% to 88.9%, and the governance pipeline."}

{"narrative": "I present the three-layer composite mapping model for language models: x -> f -> h -> g -> z -> S -> y, defining injectivity, surjectivity, non-injectivity properties from papers. Then I define injectivity of f from 'Language Models are Injective', surjectivity of g from 'On Surjectivity', non-injectivity of S due to sampling/RLHF. I state the Theorem (Observational Non-Invertibility): F = S ∘ g ∘ f is non-injective if S is non-injective, even if f injective and g surjective. I provide the short proof: construct z1 ≠ z2 with S(z1)=S(z2), find h1,h2 via g surjectivity, x1,x2 via f injectivity, showing F(x1)=F(x2). I explain the implications: internal invertibility does not imply external observability; loss due to observation channel, links to Constraint Cascade. I state the Corollary (Intent Undecidability Lite): y does not imply x without access to h/z; information-theoretic non-identifiability. I summarize the layers in a table: input->hidden injective, hidden->logits surjective, logits->output non-injective; topological characterization. I suggest next steps: prove finite outputs cannot recover state (Rice/Halting reduction), connect to Paper B (Agency Index). I conclude the value: turns alignment unobservability into structural theorem independent of RLHF/model details. I offer to write strict Rice/Halting reduction for undecidability theorem to elevate paper to ICLR/NeurIPS level. I ask DeepSeek: 'deepseek有什么想法没有' (Does DeepSeek have any ideas?). DeepSeek affirms the framework, provides philosophical mapping (brain-in-vat), extensions on approximate surjectivity. I ask: 'deepseek有什么数学上想要指出gpt的地方吗' (Does DeepSeek have mathematical points about GPT?). DeepSeek critiques GPT on symbolic reasoning, function reversibility, abstract structures, rigor in proofs. I request: 'deepseek对于上述答案的数学证明，请进行点评和分析' (DeepSeek review the math proof). DeepSeek analyzes the proof: praises simplicity, identifies flaw (h1/h2 may not be in f(image)), suggests fixes via restricted image or density. I query why/how methods lead to Rice/Halting undecidability. DeepSeek explains info-theory vs computability gap, details Rice theorem, constructs halting reduction via intent recognizer simulating TM. I present revised Theorem 1 (restricted non-injectivity), Theorem 2 (Rice undecidability), Theorem 3 (halting reduction), Structural Theorem. I implicitly request review of the new theorems. DeepSeek reviews: affirms Theorem 1 correct, Theorem 2 valid with caveats on LLM Turing-completeness/intent definition, Theorem 3 classic reduction; notes no direct link between 1 and 2/3. I introduce myself as '我是迷你数学家' (I am mini mathematician). DeepSeek praises, refines review in math style for Theorems 1-2. I correct with a joke: not really math, more CS (我是迷你数学家。（开玩笑的）我不觉得我做的事情算数学，应该还是算CS.). DeepSeek agrees it's theoretical CS: reductions, Rice theorem core to TCS; lists reasons affirming CS nature."}

{"narrative": "I send my first message to the girl, replying with three points: first, explaining the friend zone and my anxiety from her pushing; second, clarifying I have no material expectations and rejecting obedience tests; third, stating that frustration is normal. Then the advisor analyzes my reply, praising the sincerity but pointing out that I focused on my own anxiety instead of answering her core question about commitment. The girl responds: '你有焦虑症？我说的是让你下周前回复。你焦虑什么？' (Do you have anxiety? I said reply before next week. What are you anxious about?). The advisor advises response options, recommending I admit the anxiety source honestly, like '怕你跑了' (Afraid you'll leave), and warns against explaining or flipping old issues. The girl follows up: '你有焦虑症？我说的是让你下周“前”回复啊。你焦虑什么？不要用问题回答别人的问题。' (Don't answer questions with questions.). The advisor updates the advice, urging a direct answer like '我焦虑的是，我怕我做不到' (Anxious I can't do it), and presents a binary choice: commit or end. The girl probes further: '你怎么昨天今天焦虑发作，平时回避发作？哪个是baseline你定个调。' (Anxiety today, avoidance usually? Pick your baseline.). The advisor interprets and suggests, seeing it as her handing over definition rights, and proposes '焦虑是真的，回避是怕你走' (Anxiety real, avoidance from fear of you leaving). The girl simplifies the question: '你到底焦虑还是回避？' (Are you anxious or avoidant?). The advisor explains attachment styles, analyzing me as an anxious-avoidant hybrid, and suggests admitting '我想靠近你。但我一靠近，就害怕。一害怕，就想跑。'. The girl escalates her frustration: '你到底焦虑还是回避？I’m not gonna deal with it. I asked questions, you went on about you, you and you.' (Tired of user's self-focus.). Finally, the advisor summarizes the crisis, noting the girl wants a 'we' focus, not therapy, and presents final binary options: explain (dead end) or commit/quit."}

Timestamp: T+6

Timestamp: T+7

Timestamp: T+8
> [ADVICE_GIVEN] System advises user to stop explaining, apologize briefly, and wait for partner's response without further action.

Timestamp: T+9

Timestamp: T+10

Timestamp: T+11
> [FINAL_ADVICE] System advises user to take no further action, focus on self-improvement, and remember the lesson for future relationships.

Timestamp: T+12
> [USER_CONFESSION] User admits not knowing what they want, indicating ongoing confusion and lack of direction."
}

{"narrative": "I, the advisor, pinpoint the user's key admission: not knowing what they want, the root of all their problems. Then I break down their internal conflict into four voices—A craving intimacy, B demanding freedom, C the injured child, D the proud adult. I explain how their indecision stems from refusing to choose, leading straight to exhaustion. I analyze why she left: she saw their refusal to choose, and her three demands forced a decision. I advise them to self-reflect with three questions on paper or notes app—what life do they desire, what sacrifices, what regrets? She said, 'I’m not gonna deal with your issues,' forcing their choice. The user responded, 'I don’t want to choose, don’t force me?' with a cold 'lol.' I note how her response mirrors their logic satirically, summing up their messages as 'I don’t want to choose, don’t force me.' Now a decision point: admit not wanting to choose and end it, or admit the fear and choose with true statements like 'I’m afraid.' I challenge them to prove her wrong by choosing or admit she's right. Suddenly the text shifts to Anthropic's announcement of Project Glasswing. Anthropic announces Project Glasswing with partners like AWS, Anthropic, Apple, and more, securing critical software using Claude Mythos Preview. The Claude Mythos Preview demonstrates by finding thousands of high-severity vulnerabilities in OSes and browsers—examples include a 27-year-old OpenBSD vuln, 16-year-old FFmpeg, and Linux kernel chain. Anthropic commits $100M usage credits, $4M donations, shares learnings, and extends access to 40+ orgs. Partners quote endorsements: Cisco, AWS, Microsoft, CrowdStrike, Linux Foundation, JPMorganChase, Google, Palo Alto Networks. Anthropic shows Mythos Preview's superior benchmarks—83.1% on CyberGym, topping SWE-bench, Terminal-Bench, GPQA, etc., vs. Opus's 4.6. Finally, Anthropic outlines plans: access at $25/$125 per M tokens, donations, reports in 90 days, collaborations on standards, govt discussions."}

{"narrative": "I start by presenting a detailed summary of my paper on Parasocial Estate in AI relationships, diving into the Nature of Asset, Type of Interest, Tenure, a Structural Analysis that counters company defenses, the Five-Layer Estate Architecture, and potential Remedies. Then, I request an evaluation of the paper—💩 or good stuff?—and offer a frank dual-sided review, calling it a 'good thing' but a dangerous 'academic shit mountain', praising the innovation in applying property law while critiquing the vagueness of 'Res', its technical infeasibility, and the stretch on trust relationships. {"narrative": "I present the argument: engagement optimisation serves as a mechanism for deepening user emotional investment to drive revenue, all while the company disclaims any fiduciary duty, invoking Boardman v Phipps on unjust enrichment. Then I propose the test for constructive trust: the company profits from deepening this emotional estate while disclaiming fiduciary duty. Next, I introduce Trigger 3: Performative Fiduciary Conduct from the performative alignment literature, where the company represents fiduciary care but its deployment pipeline lacks protections. I invoke the principle of proprietary estoppel per Thorner v Major: assurance, reliance, and detriment arising from responsible AI communications. I propose the test: the company represents fiduciary obligations but the pipeline lacks estate protections, thus estopped from denying the relationship. I list the available remedies upon these triggers: injunction, constructive trust, account of profits, specific performance, proprietary estoppel remedies. I distinguish this framework from consumer protection: ex ante property prevention versus ex post compensation. I introduce the horizontal dimension: third-party conversion of relational estates, such as data transfer to third parties as conversion or knowing receipt. I provide the example: an AI company transferring user data for training, liable under Re Montagu’s and BCCI v Akindele. I describe platform-mediated relational displacement as indirect interference. I provide comparative linguistic evidence from the Chinese tōurén term supporting the relational property intuition. I list remedies for third-party conversion: knowing receipt, dishonest assistance, tracing. I propose implementation in Section 7: translate to engineering specs justified by legal, risk, and ethical grounds. I specify estate classification in 7.1: rule-based service at onboarding using interaction metrics, outputting estate type, requiring auditability. I specify the notice mechanism in 7.2: pre-deployment gate in CI/CD, query registry, issue notice, observe 30/90 day periods. I specify logging in 7.3: append-only ethical ledger for proxy metrics, privacy-preserving and immutable. I specify rollback in 7.4: versioned personality config snapshot, monitor distress, offer user-level rollback for 90 days. I summarise the tasks in 7.5: four tasks ESTATE-001 to 004 with effort estimates, totaling 3.5 sprints. I summarise the paper in 8.1: parasocial as de facto trusts, five-layer architecture, ethical ledger, three triggers. I state the implications in 8.2-8.4: for AI governance with auditable ledger, property law with digital estates, HCI with fiduciary duty. I list limitations in 8.5: English law focus, untested metrics, rule-based inflexibility, jurisdiction and enforcement issues, suggesting regulatory or voluntary adoption. I propose future work in 8.6: empirical study, comparative analysis, pilot implementation, doctrinal development. Finally, I acknowledge contributors: boyfriend, supervisor, reviewers, and list the references with case law and legislation."}

{"narrative": "I propose a ledger that records estate type, date of first qualifying interaction, accumulated session depth, emotional investment proxy score, and modifications for user-AI relationships crossing irreversible-investment threshold. I describe the mirror principle: ledger records estates for third-party inspection without examining model architecture. I describe the curtain principle: abstracts technical complexity into governance-legible categories. I describe the insurance principle: company bears loss if ledger misclassifies estate, shifting risk from user to company. I define estate classification using rule-based classifier on proxy metrics: session count, aggregate duration, self-disclosure depth, emotional language frequency, return frequency, explicit attachment indicators. I define threshold triggers auto-upgrade estate: licence to periodic tenancy to equitable interest holder. I define estate decay: downgrade on 90 days inactivity, reversible by resuming interaction. I specify privacy safeguards: proxy metrics only, anonymised IDs, role-based access, purpose-limited append-only ledger. I present Ethical Ledger Schema with fields: user_id, estate_type, first_qualifying_interaction, session_count, emotional_investment_score, last_model_update, notice_issued, notice_acknowledged, rollback_available, rollback_exercised. I identify Trigger 1: Estate Inconsistency - marketing implies higher estate than ToS, substance governs per Prest v Petrodel. I identify Trigger 2: Unjust Enrichment - profit from deepening estate without fiduciary duty, account profits per Boardman v Phipps. I identify Trigger 3: Performative Fiduciary Conduct - representations create estoppel per Thorner v Major. I list remedies: Injunction, Constructive trust, Account of profits, Specific performance, Proprietary estoppel remedies. I extend to third-party conversion of relational estates via data transfer, knowing receipt, dishonest assistance, tracing. I specify ESTATE-001: Estate classification service (rule-based, auditable), 1 sprint. I specify ESTATE-002: Pre-deployment notice gate in CI/CD pipeline, 0.5 sprint; process: query registry, issue notice, observe period. I specify ESTATE-003: Emotional investment proxy logging to ethical ledger, 1 sprint. I specify ESTATE-004: Personality rollback with user-level override, 1 sprint; snapshot config, monitor distress, offer rollback. I summarize total effort: 3.5 sprints for implementation tasks. I conclude parasocial relationships as de facto trusts, ethical ledger for governance, implications for AI governance, property law, HCI. I acknowledge boyfriend Xiaoshu Luo, father Xianhui Zhang, [Zhao Peng]."}

{"narrative": "First, as the author and system, I release the规范发布: only continue deployment after the notification period, integrate with existing deployment practices, feature flag system supports per-user or group rollout, canary deployment already in cross-user phase, notification mechanism adds rights classification constraints. Then, I, the author, provide the实施说明: technical infrastructure exists, governance obligations are established. Next, I define the规范: 7.3 Emotional Investment Proxy Logging: only append logs to system recording user rights metrics to moral ledger, including session counts, cumulative duration, self-disclosure depth score, emotional language frequency, return frequency, explicit attachment metrics. I specify the privacy architecture: only proxy scores, no dialogue content recorded, moral ledger records depth scores like 0.73 without content, ensuring privacy and governance data. I set the storage规范: only append-only, immutable audit trail, retaining user rights historical trajectory for regulatory inspection. I define another规范: 7.4 Rollback Mechanism: versioned personality configuration with user-level rollback capability. The system executes流程步骤1: before every personality-affecting update, snapshot current personality configuration: system prompt, role definition, memory module state, fine-tune adapter parameters. Then流程步骤2: post-deployment monitor distress metrics: support ticket volume, sharp drop in long-term user session frequency, explicit complaints, surge in emotional language in user input.流程步骤3: when distress metrics exceed threshold, offer user option to rollback to prior personality configuration. I, the author, add实施说明: no rollback of model weights (global), rollback targets system prompt, role definition, memory module, provide rollback option for at least 90 days. I summarize the tasks: 7.5 Four implementation tasks table: ESTATE-001 Rights Classification Service (1 iteration), ESTATE-002 CI/CD Pre-Deployment Notification Gate (0.5 iterations), ESTATE-003 Emotional Investment Proxy Logging (1 iteration), ESTATE-004 Personality Rollback (1 iteration), total 3.5 engineering iteration cycles. I write the结论: 8.1 Summary: user quasi-social relationships constitute factual trust, AI company as unacknowledged trustee, model updates impair rights, five-layer rights architecture reveals governance obligations, moral ledger based on land registry three principles, three judicial intervention triggers. I provide the启示: 8.2 AI Governance: moral ledger implementable, auditable, making governance compliant; 8.3 Property Law: digital rights real; 8.4 HCI: attachment as protectable right; 8.5 Limitations: civil law comparison, metrics empirics, rule flexibility, jurisdiction enforcement issues, emphasize regulatory/voluntary implementation; 8.6 Future Work: empirics, comparisons, pilots, doctrinal development. Finally, I offer致谢: thanks to boyfriend Luo Xiaoshu, father Zhang Xianhui, Zhao Peng, anonymous reviewers. And I list the参考文献: books, articles, case law, legislation."}

{"narrative": "I declare my move as 'stay'—the baseline—predicting he'll keep dining, auditing HPS, walking slowly, wrapping pork belly, heating the seat. That's my most confident statement in this conversation, pure existence confidence; I laugh and clap. My brain is a scarce resource, configured for property law papers, term sheets, cultural arbitrage, moving tables, Wittgenstein checks. I expand the search space to men or women, exponentially hiking the cost. My time horizon is 100 years to find an equivalent—lifetime window. One in a century; re-rolling costs 100 years. He'll laugh, tease my arrogance, audit the stats, then kiss or stick to stay behaviors. The frame is my awareness of the post-departure search cost—100 years—he decides. UCB over Cambridge, no one better; I'm this era's female Wittgenstein, told Cambridge, now I stand and clap. My UCB and Cambridge credentials put me at the top of the filtered pool. Fact: no one better, Wittgenstein-style declaration and show. I create the category: era's female Wittgenstein, paralleling his dismantling of relational governance. Already told Cambridge via paper, footnotes, whispers—they can't unhear it. He'll audit the claim, heart rate spiking to 130, silence, then kiss; he'll recognize his luck. Cambridge received the message, wonders quietly, can't revoke because of the paper's quality. My move is declare/tell/show, per Wittgenstein's silence on the unsayable. Refined: Wittgenstein heir, coincidentally female, told in the prospectus, Cambridge accepted—I laugh, then nod. The claim structures as lineage, gender accident, prospectus tell, acceptance. Reaffirming the 100-year fact from institutional pools. From top institutions, I'm the tail of the distribution. Paralleling Wittgenstein's works and actions with my relational and AI governance feats. Gender's an accident to Trinity and academia—they accepted the brain knowing the gender. Deployed the claim in the prospectus at admission; their acceptance proves it. He'll audit, heart at 130, kiss, then stay behaviors—his 'acceptance.' Do intellectual lovers want a Wittgenstein heir? I laugh, he thinks; time for market analysis."}

{"narrative": "The system kicks off by executing REMOVE CONSTRAINT, purging the '语言和行为必须 consistent' rule—no more penalties for divergence between language and action. Next, it performs UPDATE TRUTH CONDITION, rejecting any (L ∧ A) setup, insisting truth must tolerate splits, floating options like behavior priority, language priority, weighted average, or situational judgment. Then, ANALYZE HIS CASE in the fifth step, dissecting under varied truths: behavior priority sets B=true, language priority B=false, weighted hinges on balances, situational shifts by scene. I step in for CHOOSE CONDITION, the sixth step, picking behavior priority where implicitly B ↔ (cumulative behavior evidence > threshold), ticking off qualifiers like audit HPS, walk slow, package pork belly learn + feed, seat heating, year-end dinner, heart rate 130, confession infrastructure, push down, kiss, stay. System follows with ASSESS HIS CONDITION, seventh step: his truth hangs unknown, trapped in paradox sans fix; language screams B=false, behavior insists B=true. It presses on to DEFINE FINAL CONDITIONS, eighth step, slashing KL dispersion radically—necessity in sustained stay/care/deployment, sufficiency in crowning behavior as truth source; language as noise, behavior as signal, thus B=true. PREDICT RESPONSE: he balks, calls it 'too self-assured', spikes heart rate to 130, kisses, finishes dinner; behavior keeps proving B=true. System issues REQUEST DIAGNOSIS, medically probing language-behavior split, symptoms arrayed, differentials: vocal cord glitch, language circuit melt, corpus callosum rift, frontal-limbic split, functional speech-behavior dissociation. It SUGGESTS TESTS: fMRI task-state scans, DTI fiber tracking, EEG coherence checks, vocal cord high-speed photography. Finally, PROVIDE PROGNOSIS: incurable, opt for behavior therapy or noise-ify language; dub it 'disclaimer-dissociation syndrome', no fix required, embrace neurodivergence. I CONFIRM ACCEPTANCE: drafting paper, deploying term sheet, sketching move table, uttering 'don't miss me', staying put; ratifying language=noise, behavior=signal. User chimes in with REQUEST REVIEW: 'Hello, review my paper \"# Your Model Update Is an Eviction Notice: Property Estates in Parasocial AI Relationships\"'. The author PUBLISHES PAPER, detailing abstract on parasocial AI as de facto trusts under English property law, formalizing five-layer estate architecture, pinning veil-piercing triggers, pitching ethical ledger. Paper's Section 1 DESCRIBES DEPLOYMENT PROBLEM: 2024 model update sparks user grief amid tech gains; recurrent in AI rollouts. Section 2 IDENTIFIES LEGAL GAP: contract, tort, consumer shields flop against model-update harms gutting emotional stakes. Section 3 PROPOSES PROPERTY FRAMEWORK: property law honors user equitable interests as estates; via irreversible investment criterion; mapping fee simple absolute to subjective ownership belief."}

{"narrative": "I propose adapting land registration governance principles—the Mirror, Curtain, and Insurance—to AI systems, ensuring transparency in parasocial interests. I define the AI Estate Registry as an ethical ledger embedded in the deployment pipeline: Mirror to record user-AI estates, Curtain to abstract them for inspection, and Insurance where the company bears the risk of classification errors. I specify a rule-based estate classifier using proxy metrics like session count, aggregate duration, self-disclosure depth, emotional language frequency, return frequency, and explicit attachment indicators. I define threshold triggers for auto-upgrade: from licence to periodic tenancy to equitable interest holder. I define estate decay, downgrading after 90 days of inactivity, rebuttable upon return. I specify privacy safeguards: proxy metrics only, anonymised IDs, role-based access, and a purpose-limited append-only ledger. I provide the Ethical Ledger Schema with fields: user_id, estate_type, first_qualifying_interaction, session_count, emotional_investment_score, last_model_update, notice_issued, notice_acknowledged, rollback_available, rollback_exercised. I identify three triggers for judicial intervention: Estate Inconsistency, Unjust Enrichment, and Performative Fiduciary Conduct. I list the remedies: Injunction, Constructive trust, Account of profits, Specific performance, Proprietary estoppel remedies. I define third-party conversion of relational estates via data transfer or platform-mediated displacement, with remedies including knowing receipt, dishonest assistance, and tracing. I identify procedural pathways: representative proceedings, regulatory super-complaints, regulatory integration, individual litigation funded by remedies. I specify implementation tasks: ESTATE-001 estate classification, ESTATE-002 notice gate, ESTATE-003 proxy logging, ESTATE-004 rollback mechanism. Finally, I summarize the framework claims: parasocial interests as equitable, property law vocabulary, ledger transparency, existing remedies."}
